title: Progress in the theory of CMC surfaces in locally homgeneous 3-manifolds X
speaker: William Meeks
affiliation: University of Massachusetts; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Abstract: I will go over some recent work that I have been involved in on surface geometry in complete locally homogeneous 3-manifolds X. In joint work with Mira, Perez and Ros, we have been able to finish a long term project related to the Hopf uniqueness/existence problem for CMC spheres in any such X. In joint work with Tinaglia on curvature and area estimates for CMC H>0 surfaces in such an X, we have been working on getting the best curvature and area estimates for constant mean curvature estimates in terms of their injectivity radii and their genus.
tags: Workshop on Mean Curvature and Regularity

proof_stages.label: Homogeneous 3-manifold and critical-curvature framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up simply connected homogeneous 3-manifolds via the classification into S^2 x R and metric Lie groups, describes semidirect-product Lie groups, and defines the critical mean curvature H(X) as an infimum over immersed surfaces.

proof_stages.label: Cheeger identification through foliations and isoperimetry
proof_stages.summary: For noncompact simply connected homogeneous spaces, the speaker explains the theorem identifying the Cheeger constant with twice the critical mean curvature, using special CMC plane foliations, large isoperimetric domains, the asymptotic slope of the isoperimetric profile, and maximum-principle barriers.

proof_stages.label: Hopf-type classification statement for CMC spheres
proof_stages.summary: The main Hopf uniqueness/existence theorem is stated: CMC spheres are unique up to congruence or left translation at a fixed absolute mean curvature, the allowed H-values are all real values outside the R^3-type case and |H|>H(X) in the R^3-type case, and the spheres have precise index, nullity, Alexandrov embeddedness, and symmetry properties.

proof_stages.label: Lie-group Gauss map, Jacobi fields, and local moduli
proof_stages.summary: After fixing a Lie group structure on the universal cover, right-invariant Killing fields give three independent Jacobi functions, Cheng’s theorem forces nullity exactly three for index-one spheres, and the implicit function theorem yields a one-dimensional local family parametrized by mean curvature.

proof_stages.label: Left-invariant Gauss-map diffeomorphism and curvature estimates
proof_stages.summary: A representation theorem and nodal-domain argument show that the left-translation Gauss map of an index-one CMC sphere is a degree-one diffeomorphism, and this degree constraint rules out curvature blow-up by comparison with the negative local Gauss-map degree of a blown-up minimal surface in R^3.

proof_stages.label: Area estimates and degeneration alternatives
proof_stages.summary: The proof analyzes sequences of CMC spheres with unbounded area: in the SU(2) case stable right-invariant limit surfaces contradict quadratic-area-growth Jacobi-function constraints, while in the R^3-type case degeneration can occur only as H approaches the critical value and the limiting geometry becomes the critical CMC plane foliation.

proof_stages.label: Global uniqueness and completion of the sphere theorem
proof_stages.summary: Curvature and area estimates make the moduli components intervals; comparison of two same-H spheres via their Gauss-map parametrizations produces a complex quadratic differential with forbidden negative-index zeros on S^2, forcing a single left-translation class and completing the classification and Alexandrov-embeddedness conclusions.

proof_stages.label: Curvature estimates for embedded H-disks
proof_stages.summary: A second theorem is outlined: if embedded H-disks in a locally homogeneous 3-manifold had unbounded curvature away from the boundary with H bounded below, a blow-up would yield a bounded-curvature genus-zero CMC planar domain in R^3; dynamics and CMC flux then rule out the Delaunay/Riemann/catenoid alternatives, leaving only the helicoid-type limiting scenario needed for the estimate.

examples.name: Hyperbolic 3-space and horosphere foliations
examples.note: Hyperbolic space is used to make the critical-mean-curvature/Cheeger formula concrete: its Cheeger constant is 2, so the critical mean curvature is 1, realized visually by horospheres.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes the Cheeger constant of hyperbolic 3-space as 2, infers critical mean curvature 1, and identifies this with the horosphere foliation

examples.name: Euclidean Hopf theorem and round spheres
examples.note: The classical fact that CMC spheres in R^3 are round is used as the motivating model for the Hopf uniqueness problem and later as the familiar case where areas blow up as H approaches 0.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites Hopf’s Euclidean theorem that CMC spheres in R^3 are round and notes their uniqueness up to translation, without deriving or applying it concretely

passes.reason: Round spheres in Euclidean space are the highly symmetric model CMC spheres in Hopf’s theorem.

examples.name: Berger-sphere embeddedness behavior
examples.note: In response to a question, Berger-type left-invariant metrics on SU(2) illustrate that the CMC spheres supplied by the theorem may be embedded for some mean curvatures and not embedded for others.

examples.name: Isoperimetric conjectures in SU(2) and metric R^3 groups
examples.note: A question about index-one versus isoperimetric behavior prompts the aside that isoperimetric regions in SU(2) may be spheres or tori, while in metric R^3 groups they are conjecturally unique spheres foliating the punctured space.
examples.engagement_reason: he states conjectural isoperimetric behavior in SU(2) and metric R^3 groups, such as spheres or tori and uniqueness, without working through the conjectures
